metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` / `pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport` capability assertion *before* the epic is accepted: either (a) show a completed spike result (importable module, working transport injection) checked into the repo, or (b) name an explicit fallback dependency (`httpx-retrying` or a hand-rolled tenacity wrapper) if the extra is unavailable, and update AC#8 accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` is a confirmed real extra at v1.107.0 shipping `pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport` and `wait_retry_after`, and names it as a settled dependency in AC#8 — but this claim is unresolved in the codebase (the existing `[agents]` extra uses only `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic]`, no `[retries]` appears anywhere in pyproject.toml or src/), and the sole de-risking mechanism is a *future* child spike story (95a5). Prior cached review passes uniformly flagged this extra as nonexistent; the plan rebuts them as 'code-blind' but provides only a shell command (not a completed spike result) as evidence.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point' section; AC#8

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Harden the spike (story 95a5) as a hard sequential gate — not 'belt-and-suspenders' — and amend the plan's 'confirmed' language to 'to-be-confirmed by spike 95a5'; include in the spike's AC: (a) successful `import pydantic_ai.retries; pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport` at pydantic-ai 1.107.0, (b) `pip install pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` installs tenacity as a transitive dep (verify with `pip show tenacity`), and (c) a 429→200 retry observed below the agent graph with no tool re-execution.
findings.finding: The plan declares `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` and `pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport` + `wait_retry_after` as 'reproducibly verified' confirmed facts, but zero codebase artifacts support this claim: `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` does not appear in pyproject.toml, `pydantic_ai.retries` / `AsyncTenacityTransport` / `wait_retry_after` have no matches anywhere in the repo, and `tenacity` is absent as a dependency. The plan simultaneously says the spike (story 95a5) is 'REQUIRED BEFORE dependent work builds on it,' which logically contradicts a 'confirmed' claim. The 'confirmed' framing risks child-story authors treating the spike as optional, or proceeding in parallel, when it should be a hard gate. If `[retries]` is not a real extra, `pip install pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` silently no-ops in some pip versions, `tenacity` is never installed, and the central self-healing mechanism fails at runtime with a cold `ImportError`.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point (reproducibly verified)'; AC#8: 'pydantic-ai-slim[retries] is added as a dependency'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point (reproducibly verified)' to 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point (to be confirmed by spike 95a5 before any dependent story begins).' Add to the spike's AC the three checks above. Remove the 'false-positive from a code-blind reviewer' dismissal — it preemptively discredits legitimate T3 scrutiny.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to spike 95a5's AC an end-to-end proof that `AnthropicProvider(http_client=AsyncTenacityTransport(...))` actually intercepts a 429 response at the httpx level and retries it transparently below a `run_sync` call, rather than just confirming the constructor parameter name.
findings.finding: The `AnthropicProvider(http_client=...)` injection surface — the mechanism by which `AsyncTenacityTransport` is wired into the Anthropic SDK — is cited via a `python -c inspect.signature(...)` probe in the plan text but is not corroborated by any codebase artifact. The live codebase only uses `AnthropicProvider(base_url=_direct)` (runner.py:240). If `AnthropicProvider.__init__` does not accept `http_client=` at pydantic-ai 1.107.0 (or accepts it but does not forward it to the underlying `AsyncAnthropic` client's httpx transport layer), the entire injection design is blocked and the 'no side-effect re-run' guarantee fails.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point'; Context — 'Self-healing retry at the httpx transport layer'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the 95a5 spike (or add a sibling micro-spike) to empirically verify that (a) an httpx read timeout on the async Anthropic client and (b) a per-tool timeout both interrupt a stalled `agent.run_sync()` call within the expected deadline, before dependent liveness stories build on those mechanisms.
findings.finding: The per-request read timeout and per-tool timeout mechanisms are asserted as `run_sync`-compatible but are never empirically probed. Under `agent.run_sync()`, pydantic-ai drives an async client inside an internally-managed event loop; whether an httpx read timeout (set on the async client) and a per-tool timeout (likely a `threading.Timer` or `asyncio` mechanism) actually fire and surface correctly in that context is a distinct integration question not covered by the 95a5 transport spike (which focuses on 429→200 retry, not timeout-interrupt behavior).
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'run_sync-compatible liveness' bullet; Success Criteria bullet 4; AC bullet 5
findings.suggested_fix: In the 95a5 spike story, add a second fixture: a mock transport that sleeps indefinitely, assert that run_sync raises/surfaces a timeout within N seconds. For per-tool timeout, add a mock tool that blocks, assert the tool-level deadline fires. Document the chosen timeout-interrupt mechanism (threading.Timer vs signal vs asyncio) as an ADR note.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the gate-wiring story, explicitly specify the verdict-dict key (e.g. `coverage.resolution_class`) that all three gate CLIs test to emit exit 11, enumerate all three CLI paths that need updating (`_review_plan`, `_verify_completion`, and the transition/completion-gate path), and add that mapping as a named deliverable in the story's AC.
findings.finding: The mechanism by which a systemic-retryable failure signals exit 11 to the CLI is under-specified across all three gate CLIs. The plan states exit 11 is 'bifurcated by the resolution_class carried on the verdict coverage,' but does not define: (a) whether the verdict dict grows a new top-level or coverage-nested key the CLI can test, (b) that _verify_completion (currently `0 if verdict == PASS else 1`, no INDETERMINATE case) and the transition/completion-gate CLI path each require explicit exit-mapping updates, or (c) how resolution_class flows from the raised exception through the degraded-verdict construction into the dict the CLI tests. Without this specificity in the gate-wiring story's scope the implementer may correctly build exit 11 for plan-review but leave the other gate CLIs silently emitting exit 1 for a systemic-retryable failure.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions ('Exit-code signal' bullet); AC line 4 ('all three gates share the two-scope posture'); src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py:383,297

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the exit-2 label contradiction in Architecture constraints: pick one authoritative definition ('usage error' or 'INDETERMINATE') for exit 2, remove the conflicting label, and ensure both bullets use the same terminology — then verify the AC exit-11 wiring story references the corrected definition.
findings.finding: Within the Architecture constraints section, exit code 2 is simultaneously labeled 'INDETERMINATE exit 2' and 'exit 2 = usage error, NOT INDETERMINATE' — a direct contradiction that would send an implementer in two directions when deciding what exit code currently represents INDETERMINATE and what the baseline for the new exit-11 distinction is.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'Exit code 11 amends a FROZEN contract' bullet and 'Exit-code signal' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: If exit 2 is the *current* INDETERMINATE signal being replaced by exit 11, say so explicitly ('exit 2 previously served as INDETERMINATE; exit 11 is its dedicated replacement'). If exit 2 is strictly a usage-error code, remove 'INDETERMINATE exit 2' from the Exit-code signal bullet and name the actual prior INDETERMINATE exit code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify the session log write path file in 2eca's Files list (or confirm that the session log is the verdict file itself), and add an explicit AC sub-point: 'the session log write path is identified and the diagnostic is verified to appear in operator-visible session log output.'
findings.finding: Child 2eca's AC requires `resolution_class` + sanitized `diagnostic` to be plumbed through the 'session log' as a named sink, but no child's Files list includes the session log write path, and no child's AC explicitly owns the session-log plumbing — this may be an owned-by-none implementation gap.
findings.location: child 2eca-0a1d-45c2-4e38 (Acceptance Criteria: 'resolution_class + sanitized diagnostic are plumbed through the verdict schemas + coverage + the session log')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate a single owner for the diagnostic-sanitization ADR (recommend 30ff, since it owns the concrete mechanism), and change 2eca's AC to say 'diagnostic-sanitization ADR produced by 30ff; 2eca references it' rather than claiming it as a 2eca deliverable.
findings.finding: Both child 30ff and child 2eca independently claim ownership of the diagnostic-sanitization ADR: 30ff says 'The ADR records the allowlist + pattern table as the contract' and 2eca's AC lists 'ADRs recorded (exit-11 contract, diagnostic sanitization, deferred async watchdog, scope-vs-title)' as a deliverable — two children are accountable for producing the same singleton artifact.
findings.location: child 30ff-21a3-0724-422b (What / Diagnostic sanitization) and child 2eca-0a1d-45c2-4e38 (AC final bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: In 2eca's AC, replace 'diagnostic sanitization' in the ADR list with 'diagnostic-sanitization ADR owned by 30ff — 2eca references it in the consolidated docs'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove 'scope-vs-title' from 2eca's ADR list in the AC, replacing it with a note: 'scope-vs-title ADR waived per parent plan (title broadened to match scope).'
findings.finding: Child 2eca's AC lists 'scope-vs-title' as a required ADR deliverable, but the parent plan explicitly states 'The title was broadened to match scope, so no separate scope ADR is needed' — these are directly contradictory requirements for the same story's deliverable list.
findings.location: child 2eca-0a1d-45c2-4e38 (Acceptance Criteria, final bullet) vs parent plan (Architecture constraints)
findings.suggested_fix: Edit 2eca AC final bullet: 'ADRs recorded (exit-11 contract, deferred async watchdog); diagnostic-sanitization ADR owned by story 30ff; scope-vs-title ADR waived per parent plan.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Unify the name for the failure-classification field throughout the plan: choose either `resolution_class` or `resolution_disposition` (or the full enum name) and apply it consistently in the design bullets, Architecture constraints, and AC line 2.
findings.finding: The concept of the failure-classification type is named inconsistently: 'resolution-disposition set/enum' in the design bullets and AC, but 'resolution_class' (a different identifier) in the Architecture constraints section — an implementer must guess whether these are the same construct or two different fields.
findings.location: Design bullets ('Classify what survives retries into a closed resolution-disposition set'); AC line 2 ('closed resolution-disposition enum'); Architecture constraints ('Exit-code signal' bullet: 'the `resolution_class` carried on the verdict `coverage`')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'resolution-disposition set/enum' in the design bullet and AC with `resolution_class` (matching the Architecture constraints reference), or vice versa — whichever matches the planned code identifier.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify or remove 'the MCP error shape' from 2eca's What section: either define what MCP error shape means in this context and add it to the AC, or delete it as a residual artifact from an earlier design iteration.
findings.finding: Child 2eca's What section mentions 'the MCP error shape' as a deliverable alongside 'per-class actionable messages' and 'exit code 11', but this term appears nowhere in the parent plan, in any sibling story, or in 2eca's own AC — it is an unexplained residual reference that may represent a hidden undocumented requirement.
findings.location: child 2eca-0a1d-45c2-4e38 (What, final bullet: 'Per-class actionable messages; the MCP error shape; exit code 11.')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert that `resolution_class` is added as an optional/nullable field with a documented default (e.g., `None`/absent ≡ pre-feature behaviour), confirm all verdict readers tolerate its absence, and state the post-rollback schema state explicitly (e.g., 'rolled-back readers ignore unknown fields per the existing schema policy, no file scrub needed') — or add a compensating-forward migration note if strict readers exist.
findings.finding: The plan adds a new `resolution_class` field to the persisted verdict `coverage` schema but makes no assertion about forward/backward compatibility of already-written verdict files, nor about the schema state after rollback. If the change is partially deployed and then rolled back, verdict files written during the forward window will contain `resolution_class` fields that rolled-back readers never declared; the plan is silent on whether readers tolerate unknown fields, whether a compensating forward-path exists, or whether those files must be scrubbed/ignored on rollback.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'Exit-code signal' paragraph + AC line: 'Exit-11 frozen-contract change is handled'; also Architecture constraints: 'the verdict schemas…are touched by multiple children'
findings.suggested_fix: In the gate-wiring story's verdict-schema section, add: (1) declare `resolution_class` as `Optional[ResolutionDisposition] = None` so absent-field = pre-feature behaviour; (2) confirm that all verdict consumers (completion-verify, CI, Gerrit bot) use permissive JSON parsing (additionalProperties allowed); (3) add a one-line rollback assertion: 'post-rollback, files written during forward deployment are inert — rolled-back code ignores the unknown field; no scrub required.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a spike test case for the exhausted-retry path (all attempts fail) to empirically confirm whether tenacity emits the original exception or a RetryError wrapper when reraise=True, and reconcile 95a5 and 30ff's descriptions accordingly.
findings.finding: Child 95a5 specifies `reraise=True` for the AsyncTenacityTransport (meaning the last attempt's original exception is always re-raised), but child 30ff simultaneously says 'some tenacity paths wrap in a RetryError' requiring an explicit unwrap step — these are contradictory claims about what the same transport will emit at the classifier's boundary.
findings.location: child 95a5-41eb-cffd-477b (What / Retry set) and child 30ff-21a3-0724-422b (What / Unwrap RetryError)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend 95a5's spike to include an all-fail scenario (mock always returns 429) and assert what exception type reaches the caller; if it is a RetryError, document that in 95a5 and keep 30ff's unwrap; if it is the original exception, remove the RetryError unwrap from 30ff and add a regression test asserting it never appears.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all consumers of the `sign_manifest`/attestation output (not just exit-code consumers) in 2eca's consumer-impact section, and confirm each consumer handles 'no attestation produced' (exit 11) correctly — or document a compensating artifact (e.g., an unsigned diagnostic file) for consumers that require a file to be present.
findings.finding: Child 2eca adds a structural never-sign guard that makes a degraded/INDETERMINATE verdict structurally unsigned (raises rather than producing a signed artifact), but the consumer-impact analysis does not enumerate consumers of the `sign_manifest`/attestation output that currently may expect a signed artifact to exist for any verdict outcome — the behavioral change from 'signed FAIL attestation' to 'no attestation produced' is not analyzed.
findings.location: child 2eca-0a1d-45c2-4e38 (What / Never-sign structural guard)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify exactly what is new in each 'additive output self-heal tweak' relative to `structured.py`'s existing layers 2 and 4, or remove them from the plan if they are already fully implemented.
findings.finding: The two 'additive output self-heal tweaks' (schema-guided json-repair; faulty-output-in-reask) referenced as new additions are already implemented in `structured.py` layers 2 and 4 respectively, and the plan never specifies what the delta is.
findings.location: Context — 'Silent-success layer' bullet: 'two additive output self-heal tweaks (schema-guided json-repair; faulty-output-in-reask) on top of rebar's already-strong structured.py ladder'
findings.suggested_fix: If the intent is to ensure these layers are active on the failure/reask path specifically (e.g., currently only active on the first attempt), state that explicitly. Otherwise, acknowledge they are already implemented and remove the 'additive' framing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Promote 95a5 to its own earlier merge wave (or add an explicit sub-ordering constraint) so fd1d's client-contribution cannot be merged before 95a5's transport-construction spike is confirmed green.
findings.finding: Child fd1d explicitly states it 'Depends on 95a5's spike passing (transport injection viable) before this timeout wiring lands' because fd1d contributes its httpx.Timeout into 95a5's shared AsyncClient construction — but the stated merge order '0→1→{2,3,4,5}→6' places both fd1d and 95a5 in the same unordered wave {2,3,4,5}, providing no enforcement that fd1d lands after 95a5's spike is confirmed.
findings.location: child fd1d-5f0d-4bd8-40f4 (Scope / Shared-client coordination) and child 95a5-41eb-cffd-477b
findings.suggested_fix: Split the merge wave: make 95a5 wave 2 (after the baseline a879=wave 1) and make fd1d wave 3, with an explicit gate that 95a5's spike integration test must be green before fd1d is merged.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit intra-wave ordering constraint: 30ff must land before 978d (and before 9a82, which also references the resolution_class vocabulary implicitly through its capability check routing).
findings.finding: Child 978d routes detected refusal-as-content to `resolution_class = CHANGE_INPUT` using 30ff's vocabulary, but both 978d and 30ff are placed in the same unordered merge wave {2,3,4,5} — if 978d lands before 30ff, the `resolution_class` enum it imports does not yet exist.
findings.location: child 978d-1d5d-8287-47bc (What / Refusal-as-content protocol) and child 30ff-21a3-0724-422b
findings.suggested_fix: In the merge order, split the wave: '0→1→30ff→{95a5, 978d, 9a82, fd1d}→2eca' (or whichever story defines the enum becomes the earliest in its sub-wave).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State concrete default values (or explicit min/max bounds) in the plan for: per-request read timeout, per-tool timeout, step-count cap, and Retry-After ceiling — e.g. 'read timeout 120 s, tool timeout 60 s, max steps 50, Retry-After cap 60 s' — so the AC is evaluable without executor judgment calls.
findings.finding: Concrete numeric values for per-request read timeout, per-tool timeout, step caps, and the Retry-After ceiling are never stated — only 'generous' and 'capped' — leaving an executor unable to implement or verify the liveness design without choosing undeclared defaults.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions (run_sync-compatible liveness paragraph); Acceptance Criteria bullets: 'Retry is owned at exactly one layer … Retry-After is honored and capped' and 'Agentic ops use run_sync-compatible liveness (per-request read timeout + per-tool timeout + step caps)'
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a table or bullet list in the Architecture constraints section, e.g.:
- Per-request read timeout: 120 s (large-context buffer; overridable via config)
- Per-tool timeout: 60 s
- Step cap: 50 steps per gate run
- Retry-After ceiling: 60 s (requests waiting longer than this are classified WAIT_AND_RETRY and surfaced to the operator)

Then update the AC to reference these defaults explicitly so a reviewer can grep the config and confirm.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out procedure for exit code 11: either (a) an expand-contract sequence (ship behind a feature flag, confirm all enumerated consumers handle 11, then remove flag) or (b) a concrete revert runbook (which files change, how to re-map 11→2, how to confirm consumers are restored) — acknowledgement of the break alone does not satisfy T4.
findings.finding: The exit-11 rollback plan ('rollback = revert the exit-11 mapping to the prior INDETERMINATE exit') is operationally thin. No expand-contract sequence is described (e.g., ship exit 11 behind a feature flag or alongside exit 2 first), and there is no stated back-out procedure if consumer enumeration reveals a consumer that is NOT additive-safe. The criterion requires an explicit rollback/back-out plan — acknowledgement alone is insufficient.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Exit code 11 amends a FROZEN contract' paragraph; AC line 'The exit-11 frozen-contract change is handled'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a two-phase rollout: Phase 1 — ship exit 11 as an alias alongside exit 2 (both codes emitted) with a `REBAR_LEGACY_EXIT_CODES=1` env-var that maps 11→2, allowing consumers to update; Phase 2 — remove the alias after all enumerated consumers confirm they handle 11. Document the env-var as the rollback lever.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly address verdict schema backward compat: confirm `resolution_class` is an optional/additive field (or enumerate and update schema-validating consumers), and add verdict-schema consumers to the enumeration already required for exit-code consumers.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `resolution_class` carried on the verdict `coverage` schema as a new field used by the gate-wiring story to bifurcate exit codes. The plan says verdict schemas are 'touched by multiple children' but does not address backward compatibility for external consumers that parse verdict output (e.g., CI pipelines, the Gerrit reviewer-bot, session-log consumers). Adding a new required field to a verdict schema is a potentially breaking change for schema-validating consumers; the plan neither confirms the field is optional/additive nor enumerates verdict-schema consumers.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Exit-code signal' paragraph; 'gate-wiring story is the named integrator' description

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add user-facing documentation (in the taxonomy/retry-timeout doc or a dedicated troubleshooting section) describing what the diagnostic sanitization preserves (e.g., HTTP status codes, provider error codes) and what it redacts (secrets, credentials, PII), so operators can reason about diagnostic completeness during incident response.
findings.finding: The diagnostic-sanitization contract is specified as an ADR (internal decision record), but operators who rely on logs, CLI stderr, and session logs for incident response need user-facing documentation of *what categories of data are scrubbed* and *what is preserved* — otherwise they cannot reason about the completeness of diagnostic output during an outage. The ADR alone does not cover this observability contract.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Diagnostic sanitization' paragraph; AC — 'ADRs are recorded for: ... the diagnostic-sanitization contract'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly enumerate the per-request read timeout, per-tool timeout, step-cap count, and Retry-After cap as named config keys in the gate-wiring story's config namespace table, confirm they appear in `_config_schema.py`, and list them in the docs AC (AC bullet 11).
findings.finding: The per-request read timeout duration, per-tool timeout duration, step-cap count, and Retry-After cap value are described only with qualitative adjectives ('generous', 'capped') rather than being explicitly identified as named, user-configurable keys. If any of these are compiled-in constants, operators with slow models, large contexts, or high-traffic environments cannot tune them without a code change.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'run_sync-compatible liveness' bullet; AC bullet 11 ('Docs cover … the config keys')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table in the gate-wiring story listing: `llm.request_timeout_s` (default e.g. 120), `llm.tool_timeout_s` (default e.g. 60), `llm.max_steps` (default e.g. 50), `llm.retry_after_cap_s` (default e.g. 300) — all overridable via the existing config ladder. Reference these keys in the ADR for the run_sync liveness design.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all catch-sites for `LLMUnavailableError` (internal modules + any documented public API) and explicitly state whether the exception type is preserved, wrapped, or replaced — add this as an AC in the gate-wiring story.
findings.finding: The plan replaces the current opaque `LLMUnavailableError` collapse with a classifier + new gate posture, but never enumerates internal or external callers that catch `LLMUnavailableError` by type — if any catch-site depends on receiving that specific exception, the new behavior silently breaks them.
findings.location: Context section ('every LLM failure collapses into one opaque LLMUnavailableError at src/rebar/llm/runner.py:349-361') and AC list
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC to the gate-wiring story: 'Audit all `except LLMUnavailableError` catch-sites; either preserve LLMUnavailableError as a base/wrapper type or explicitly migrate each catch-site and note it in the story diff.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document (or add a config flag for) the rollback/disable path for the transport-retry and classification layers independently of the exit-11 change — either a named config key to disable the new behavior or an explicit 'revert story N commit' procedure in the ADR.
findings.finding: The rollback story for the new retry/classification/liveness behavior (everything beyond exit-11) is absent — only the exit-11 rollback is named, leaving no documented or tested undo path for the transport-retry, classifier, and silent-success layers if they cause regressions.
findings.location: Architecture constraints section ('rollback = revert the exit-11 mapping to the prior INDETERMINATE exit')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a boolean config key (e.g., `llm.resilience.enabled`, defaulting to `true`) that short-circuits all new failure-handling code and restores the old LLMUnavailableError collapse behavior; this doubles as a staged-rollout toggle and a rollback lever.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the gate-wiring story, add an explicit enumeration step: search the codebase for all `except LLMUnavailableError` catch sites, list them, and confirm each is updated to inspect `resolution_class` (or the degraded-verdict builder propagates it automatically), with a failing test for each gate path.
findings.finding: The plan does not enumerate the 8+ existing `except LLMUnavailableError` catch sites that must be upgraded to inspect `resolution_class` for the exit-11 two-scope posture to hold. These sites (gate_dispatch.py lines ~136, 173, 505, 578; pass1.py lines ~193, 453; sizing.py lines ~229, 256) currently route ALL LLMUnavailableError instances to INDETERMINATE. If any catch site is missed, a transient-retryable (WAIT_AND_RETRY) failure silently falls through to an INDETERMINATE (exit 2) verdict, producing the same indistinguishable collapse the epic aims to fix.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions ('Two-scope gate posture' bullet); AC line 4; src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py:136,173,505,578; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/pass1.py:193,453; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sizing.py:229,256
findings.suggested_fix: A common degraded-verdict builder that always reads `resolution_class` off the exception and stamps it onto `coverage` would make the propagation automatic at all catch sites without per-site manual updates — consider centralizing `_degraded_*_verdict()` helpers to extract and forward `resolution_class`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief prior-art comparison of `AsyncTenacityTransport` (from `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]`) against `httpx-retrying` or a direct tenacity-wrapped `httpx.AsyncHTTPTransport`, and name the fallback dependency if the pydantic-ai extra is unavailable.
findings.finding: The plan selects `AsyncTenacityTransport` from `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` as the transport-retry implementation without comparing it to the established `httpx-retrying` PyPI library (a purpose-built, widely-used httpx retry transport) or to wrapping `httpx.AsyncHTTPTransport` directly with tenacity. No fallback is named if the pydantic-ai extra is unavailable.
findings.location: Context section — 'Self-healing retry at the httpx transport layer' bullet; Architecture constraints
findings.suggested_fix: Add a footnote in the Architecture constraints section: 'If `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` is confirmed unavailable, the fallback is `httpx-retrying>=0.3` (a purpose-built retry transport for httpx) wrapping `httpx.AsyncHTTPTransport`, configured with tenacity-compatible backoff and `Retry-After` header support.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the diagnostic-sanitization ADR (or the gate-wiring story) the concrete set of patterns/types to strip before any sink: at minimum, `Authorization`/`x-api-key` header values, `sk-ant-*` / `sk-*` key-prefix patterns, and any other credential-bearing fields that Anthropic SDK error bodies may echo verbatim.
findings.finding: The plan routes 'verbatim diagnostic' from raw provider error responses to multiple persistent sinks (logs, CLI stderr, session log, verdict) and declares a sanitization ADR, but never specifies WHAT constitutes a secret to strip — e.g., whether `Authorization`/`x-api-key` header values echoed in Anthropic SDK error bodies, `sk-ant-*` key fragments in error message strings, or request-URL query parameters are in scope. Without a concrete pattern list or sanitization contract, an implementer cannot know when the ADR is satisfied, leaving the risk that a raw SDK `AuthenticationError` or `APIStatusError` body (which can embed request headers verbatim) is stored in a verdict file or session log.
findings.location: Architecture constraints § 'Diagnostic sanitization' + AC item 'ADRs are recorded for: the diagnostic-sanitization contract'
findings.suggested_fix: In the ADR, enumerate the sanitization contract: (1) strip or redact any string matching `sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{20,}` or similar key prefix patterns; (2) remove `Authorization:` and `x-api-key:` header lines from any included header dumps; (3) specify that the scrubber runs as a named function (`sanitize_diagnostic(str) -> str`) tested with a fixture of known SDK error shapes before the diagnostic is attached to any verdict or log record.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a classification table (or explicit mapping in the child story that owns the classifier) specifying which error signals / HTTP status codes / exception types produce each of the 8 disposition values, co-located with or referenced from the resolution-disposition enum definition.
findings.finding: The 8-value resolution-disposition enum vocabulary is named in the plan but the classification decision rules (which HTTP status codes, exception types, or provider error signals map to which value) are never defined anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Context section — 'Classify what survives retries into a closed resolution-disposition set'; AC item 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a minimal mapping table to the Context or Architecture Constraints section, e.g.: 429/529 → WAIT_AND_RETRY (honor Retry-After); 5xx transient → RETRY_NOW; 401/403 → CHANGE_SETTINGS; 400/context-too-long → CHANGE_INPUT; timeout-after-retries → RETRY_NOW; refusal/content-filter → FIX_AGENT_DESIGN; budget-exceeded → INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS; anything else → NEEDS_INVESTIGATION.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `resolution_class` field (type, allowed values, nullable behaviour) that is added to the verdict/coverage schema, and specify the gate-wiring story's extraction logic (e.g. `if verdict.coverage.resolution_class in SYSTEMIC_CLASSES: exit(11)`) in the plan or the owning child story.
findings.finding: The plan states exit-code bifurcation is driven by `resolution_class` 'carried on the verdict `coverage`,' but the verdict/coverage schema is never amended to include a `resolution_class` field, and the extraction protocol at the gate-wiring decision point is unspecified.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'Exit-code signal' bullet; AC item 4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback action for each silent-success detection trigger: which exception is raised (or which disposition class is assigned) when refusal-as-content is detected, and what happens when the verdict schema-shape check fails.
findings.finding: The silent-success detection mechanisms (structured refusal-as-content detection, verdict schema-shape check) are named but the fallback/routing when detection fires is never specified.
findings.location: Context — 'Silent-success layer' bullet; Success Criteria item 5; AC item 6

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the concrete object/field that carries the `diagnostic` string (e.g., a new `ClassifiedLLMError(LLMUnavailableError)` with `disposition` and `diagnostic` fields, plus an optional `diagnostic` field on the verdict coverage schema), co-located with the resolution-disposition enum definition.
findings.finding: The 'verbatim `diagnostic`' paired with the resolution-disposition class is referenced throughout the plan but its concrete object/schema placement is never defined — it is unclear whether it is a field on a new exception subclass, a new verdict schema field, a separate dataclass, or all three.
findings.location: Context — 'Classify what survives retries' bullet; Architecture constraints — 'Diagnostic sanitization' bullet; AC item 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short type sketch: `@dataclass class DispositionResult: resolution_class: ResolutionDisposition; diagnostic: str` — and specify that `ClassifiedLLMError` wraps it, while the verdict schema gains an optional `llm_failure: DispositionResult | None` field on coverage.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit story-ID-to-wave mapping in 2eca's integration-ownership section (e.g., 'wave 0 = a879, wave 1 = 30ff, wave 2 = 95a5, wave 3 = fd1d, wave 4 = {978d, 9a82}, wave 6 = 2eca') to make the merge order unambiguous and enforceable.
findings.finding: The merge order '0→1→{2,3,4,5}→6' in child 2eca never maps position numbers to story IDs — it is unspecified which of the seven stories occupies positions 0, 1, and the four slots of {2,3,4,5}, making it impossible to enforce or verify the correct ordering at merge time.
findings.location: child 2eca-0a1d-45c2-4e38 (What / Integration ownership)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC line in 978d: 'Existing structured.py tests that cover the reask path are audited and updated (not deleted) to accommodate the faulty-prior-output addition; no previously-passing test is silently deleted.'
findings.finding: Child 978d modifies the reask behavior in `structured.py` (adding faulty-prior-output to the reask prompt) but does not enumerate existing tests that assert the current reask prompt structure, and does not include an AC line covering 'existing structured.py reask tests remain passing' beyond the generic `make test` green.
findings.location: child 978d-1d5d-8287-47bc (What / Output self-heal in structured.py and Acceptance Criteria)

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
